Influence of articular incongruency on coronal 
alignment after surgical fixation of lateral tibial 
plateau fractures
BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders
Received: 17 June 2025
Accepted: 8 December 2025
Cite this article as: Sim J.A., Kim H.S., 
Lee D.K. et al. Influence of articular 
incongruency on coronal alignment 
after surgical fixation of lateral tibial 
plateau fractures. BMC Musculoskelet

Influence of articular incongruency on coronal alignment after surgical 
fixation of lateral tibial plateau fractures

ARTICLE IN PRESS

ARTICLE IN PRESS
Influence of articular incongruency on coronal alignment after surgical 
fixation of lateral tibial plateau fractures 
 
- Abstract - 
Purpose : The surgical treatment of the tibial plateau fracture aims for anatomic reduction and rigid fixation. A residual 
articular step-off more  than  2mm in  the  weight-bearing  zone  has  a  high  impact  on  developing  posttraumatic 
osteoarthritis.  However,  the  correlation  between  malreduction  and  malalignment  has  not  been  studied  in  depth.  The 
purpose of this study was to investigate the correlation between postoperative articular congruency in different fracture 
zones in sagittal plane and the whole lower limb alignment after surgical treatment of the lateral tibial plateau fractures.  
Materials  and  Methods : Among 254  patients  who  were  surgically  treated  for fractures  of  tibial  plateau between 
January  2010  and  December  2022  at a single center,  67 patients  of  all  ages with  lateral  tibial  plateau  fractures 
(Schatzker  type  I-III) were selected  and  retrospectively  reviewed. All  patients  underwent  open  reduction  and  internal 
fixation  (ORIF)  via  plate  and  screw  construct  with  or  without bone augmentation procedure.  For  measuring lower 
extremity alignment variables, intra-articular step-offs in  mm and a  new  sagittal fracture  zone classification (1-3)  was 
applied to the lateral plateau post surgery and measured in simple x-ray radiographs and preoperative three-dimensional 
computed tomography. We stratified the fracture site into three zones in the sagittal plane .Malreduction was defined as 
articular step-off ≥ 2 mm. Malalignment was defined as a difference of more than 5° in hip-knee-ankle angle (HKA) to 
common physiologic values. 
Results : HKA showed strong negative correlation to postoperative articular step-off (Spearman's ρ = -0.69; p < 0.001). 
postoperative  valgisation  of  HKA  was  significantly  associated  with  increased  articular  incongruency (β = -.96,  p  < 
0.001),  especially  when  step-offs ≥ 2 mm (β = -3.77,  p  <  0.001).  Linear  regression  of  articular  step-off  and  fracture 
location  did  not  show  any  significance.  However,  13  of  24  malreduced  fractures  (54%)  were  in  zone  2.  When  the 
association between fracture location and HKA  was evaluated, only zone 2 showed significant effect on postoperative 
valgization (β = -2.68, p < 0.001). When controlling fractures in zone 1 and 3, articular incongruency located in zone 2 
showed significant association with HKA valgization (β = -.775, p < 0.001). 
Conclusion : This study highlights the importance of minimizing the articular step-off to be less than 2 mm in order to 
prevent the valgus malalignment in treating lateral tibial plateau fractures. 
   
Key Words : tibial plateau fracture, reduction, alignment 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
ARTICLE IN PRESS

ARTICLE IN PRESS
Introduction  
Tibial plateau fractures are intra-articular fractures of the knee joint accounting for 5-8% of all lower limb fractures.[1] 
Lateral  tibial  fractures  take  up  to  two-thirds  of  all  tibial  plateau  fractures.[2] The  aim  of  treatment  is  to  achieve 
anatomical reduction of the fracture to restore the joint surface and minimize the risks of post traumatic arthritis, and to 
allow early mobilization of the joint using rigid fixation.[3, 4] However, based on data regarding the level of articular 
congruity  that  is  needed  to  achieve  an  acceptable  outcome  and  a  low  risk  of  posttraumatic  osteoarthritis  are 
controversial. [5] Some authors have reported acceptable degree of articular incongruency can range  widely from 2 to 
10  mm  in  the  case  of  tibial  plateau. [6,  7] Other  studies  have  shown  step-offs  larger  than  2mm  lead  to  higher  joint 
contact pressure in lateral tibial plateau fractures.[8, 9]   
 Residual articular incongruency (step-off or depression) after a tibial plateau fracture is a risk factor for post-
traumatic  osteoarthritis,  but  its  direct  link  to  long-term  complications  is  complex  and  not  fully  substantiated  by 
evidence,  with  joint stability,  meniscus  integrity,  and  overall  coronal  alignment  being  potentially  more  significant 
predictors of outcome. While restoring anatomical alignment is a primary goal to protect cartilage, some studies show 
that even significant residual incongruity may not prevent good functional outcomes, suggesting that maintaining joint 
stability and the correct mechanical axis of the limb is crucial for preventing the progressive loading abnormalities that 
lead  to  post-traumatic  osteoarthritis[10,  11]. A  biomechanical  study  has  shown  that  valgus  malalignment  after  tibial 
fracture  leads  to  greater  contact  stress  on  the  lateral  plateau. [12] Assessment  of  a whole  lower  limb  alignment is 
measured using hip-knee-ankle angle (HKA) in coronal plane with the knee joint in full extension during weightbearing. 
During  full extension of  a  normal  knee,  tibiofemoral  contact  point  on  the  lateral  tibial  plateau  is  positioned  slightly 
anteromedial to the center of lateral articular surface.[13] We hypothesized that articular step-off in anterior or posterior 
sector of the lateral tibial plateau would alter the tibiofemoral contact point, affecting the whole lower limb alignment.  
To our  understanding,  there  is  only  a  few  studies  on  the  correlation  between  articular  incongruency  and 
malalignment, and no studies have investigated the effect of fracture location on the whole lower limb alignment.[8, 14] 
The  purpose  of  this  study  was  to  investigate  1)  the  correlation  between  postoperative  articular  congruency  and 
malalignment  and  2)  the  effect  of different  fracture  zones  in  sagittal  plane on the whole  lower  limb  alignment  after 
surgical treatment of the lateral tibial plateau fractures.  
 
Materials and methods 
The  study  protocol  was  approved  by  the Institutional  Review  Board  of  our  institution  (GAIRB2024-041)  prior  to  the 
study A  retrospective  analysis  of  prospectively  collected  data  from  consecutive  patients  who  underwent  surgical 
reduction  of  lateral  tibial  plateau  fractures between  January  2010  and  December  2022  was  conducted. For  this  study, 
254 patients who were surgically treated for fractures of tibial plateau between January 2010 and December 2022 at a 
tertiary  referral  trauma  center were  retrospectively  reviewed  after  approval  by  the  institutional  review  board.  Sixty-
seven patients  of  all  ages with  lateral  tibial  plateau  fractures  (Schatzker  type  I-III)  were  included.  Exclusion  criteria 
were as follows: 1) Schatzker type IV – VI (n = 46), 2) concomitant injury in the ipsilateral limb (n = 57), 3) previous 
fracture or replaced joint on the ipsilateral limb (n = 6), and absence of standing full-leg radiographs or less than one-
year of follow-up (n = 59), 4) the presence of the concomitant fracture in the contralateral leg (n = 19). 
 
Surgical procedure

정확하게 설명되었는지, 시혐대상자 혹은 대리인이 해당 사실을 이해하고 자유의사에 따라 진행되었는지 여부를 확인하는 자 * 시험책임자

files.name: 전문가활용보고서_신정민251020..pdf
files.text:
Rehabilitation protocol after ACL 
recon atpre-op and in hospital 
노원을지대병원류마티스내과신정민교수

Stages
Preoperative Rehabilitation stage
진단후~ 수술(4-6주)  *(Shaarani et al., Am J 
Sports Med 2013 6주, Eitzen et al. 프로그램(Br J 
Sports Med 2010) 5주로시행했고다만spetar 
ACL CPG, van Melick CPG 등에서consensus 가
없다고명시)
Prehab 4-6주는이상적이나acute case (2주안
수술의경우) 대부분논문에서제시하는effusion 
최대한감소, full passive extension, quad 
activation (slr w/o lag) 를목표로한다
Chronic case의경우바로check point 검사후
짧게term을가져간다
In-hospital stage
POD #1 ~ #3 
Post-hab stage
After discharge ~ Return to sports
이프로토콜은isolated ACL reconstruction을기본으로하며, meniscus/다른ligament/cartilage 수술이동반된경우각슬라이드하단의‘동반손상주의사항’을따라야함.

PreoperativeRehabilitation Stage
•Goals
•Quiet Knee : 통증=<3/10 effusionnon-trace
•ROM : full extension, flexion >120
•Strength : quad LSI >80 hamstring/hip aspossible
•Function : 정상heel-toe gait, stair up/down
•Neuromuscular control : single-leg stance
•Forbidden : pivot/ cutting/ jump/ deep OKC extension at high load
•Aspetar,vanMelicCPG 등에서명확히ACL specific 하게flexion,extension과quad 기준그리고quiet knee 를강조함
※ Meniscusrepair/ root/ cartilage병합시
-Prehab단계에서도deepsquatdeepflexion(>90°)은추가적금지
-특히roottear나예정된rootrepair가있으면, prehab에서deepflexion+ 체중부하운동(Deepsquat, lunge, heavylegpress)은하지않음.
※ Multi-ligament(PCL·MCL·LCL·PLC) 동반시
-PCL 손상: high-loadhamstringcurl, tibiaposteriortranslation유발하는운동은prehab에서부터제한.
-MCL/LCL/PLC: 무릎이안/밖으로휘는valgus/varusstress가걸리는운동(과도한laterallunge, cross-overstep등)은피함.

PreoperativeRehabilitation Stage –
acute Phase (week 1-2)
1.ROM & swelling control
Goal : full passive extension, flexion 120, swelling control
*Aspetar 권고수술전상태
•Cryotherapy& elevation : 20분하루3-4회무릎관절주변
•Prone hang / heel prop : 각3분3세트하루3번
•Heelslide : 3세트15회하루3번
*MGH,Brigham, 등에서20분씩하루여러번ROM 자주반복
※ Meniscusrepair예정
-Heelslide, sittingflexion은**90°이내**(Mass General meniscus repair protocol)까지만, deepflexion으로강하게당기지않음.
※ PCL 동반
-굽히는동안뒤꿈치로바닥을강하게밀어tibia를뒤로미는방식으로ROM 만들지않음.
※ MCL/LCL/PLC 동반
-ROM 운동중무릎이안/밖으로휘는valgus/varus가생기지않게주의명시
**Dose 의경우비교한RCT 는거의없으며여러review에서사용되는값을차용했으며
specificdose보다는규칙적이고꾸준한반복구조를만드는것이강조됨모든dose는환자가
통증이심하지않을정도의수준이여야하며절대적이지않음환자상태에따라20-30% 조절

PreoperativeRehabilitation Stage –
acute Phase (week 1-2)
2. Strength -CKC
Goal : quad LSI > 80
•Quad set 10sec 10회
•SLRflexabdaddextension3세트10회주5회extensor lag 없을때시행
•Mini squat (0-45) 3세트10회주3회
•Bridge 3세트10회주3회
*Shaarani 기준2-3세트8-15회주3회SLR mini squat
3. Neuromascular/ Balance
•Double leg stance on stable surface : 30초유지3세트잘되면팔가슴에교차이후eye closed
•Gait training : heel–toe / knee extension 하며보행10분3회주7회
※PCL 동반
-옆으로/엎드린자세에서무릎flexion 방향SLR은피함.
※MLKI
-Brace 착용하에straight line gait 연습만, 방향전환/side step은하지않
음.

PreoperativeRehabilitation Stage –Late 
Phase (week 3-4)
1.Strength
1.Squat progression (chair –free squat ) : 통증없을시progression 60도angle 10회3세트주3회
2.Step –up : 10-15cm 환측먼저10회3세트주3회
3.Bridge progression : double leg full set가능시single-leg bridge 8회3세트주3회
4.Hamstring curl : 10회3세트주3회
5.Clam shell : 10회3세트주3회
*shaarani
※ Meniscusrepair예정
-squat45–60°flexion이내
※ Meniscusrootlesion/repair예정
-Root병변에서는shallowsquat(0–45°).
※ Cartilagelesion
-shallowsquat(0–45°).

PreoperativeRehabilitation Stage –Late 
Phase (week 3-4)
2. Neuromascular/ Balance –pregressed
1.Single leg stance : 바닥에서single leg 30초3세트-> 쿠션위에서single leg 팔움직이며perturbation 주5회
2.Stepdown:환측다리로서고건측으로바닥에닿게내렸다올리기8회3세트주3회
3.Retro walking , side walking : 10m 3회주3회

PreoperativeRehabilitation Stage
•Preoperative Education
•수술& 재활설명: 9-12개월의긴재활계획+ RTS  시점(최소9개월이후) (Aspetar CPG, Panther Consensus)
•Prehab 의목적: 수술후회복속도, 근력,기능, 복귀시점까지모두영향을준다(Eitzen 2010, Kim 2015 등)
•수술전주의사항: 금지사항pivot / cutting / 방향전환/ 점프& 착지-> ACL 파열이후재파열, meniscus 추가손상
위험커짐
•재부상,OA 위험: 수술을해도안한사람보다OA 위험이올라감재부상줄이고근력체중관리중요성
•Prehab / rehab 대부분집에서본인이하는운동으로운동수행율이중요. 통증붓기무시하고과하게운동시문제설명
•Red flag : DVT/PE(붓기,fever, pain, dyspnea, chest pain) Infection (fever, pus, swelling) 운동중stop signal (통증5점
이상swelling increase , locking ,giving way)
•심리적준비: 대부분RTS 실패원인은두려움자신감부족(Ardern et al., Filbayet al)

PreoperativeRehabilitation Stage
•Check point
•Quiet Knee 
•통증=< 3/10 / Effusion none –trace
•Ref (Aspetar,vanMelick)
•ROM
•Full passive extension
•Flexion > 120
•Strength
•Quad , Ham LSI 80% 
•Normal heel –toe gait, 계단오르내리기가능
•Ref (van Melick 2016 CPG, GiescheSR, Kim 2012, Cunha 2022)
※Meniscus / root / cartilage 병합시
-Prehab checkpoint에서도deep squat, lunge, hop test 등은하지않고
ROM/strength 기준중심으로평가.
**Full extension, trace effusion, 정상보행확보, LSI 70 이상을최소한의기준으로본다(van Melic 
CPG, Aspetar CPG)
Isokinetic dynomamometer를통해LSI 측정이표준적인방법이나현실적인방법으로
Single –leg hop test 를사용한다
단quiet knee에서만사용해야하고acute 한손상의경우LSI 측정을skip 하고quit knee + 
ROM을목표로한다

In-hospital stage(pod#1~3) 
•Primary Goals 
•Pain & effusion control
•Graft , 상처보호
•Early ROM (full extension)
•Quad activation
•조기WB + gait 준비(*후술하듯적절한기준이중요Aspetar 권장Fan 2022 review 에서도적절한관리하에는이득이크다결론)

In-hospital stage(pod#1~3) 
•Pain&effusioncontrol
•Cryocuff/ cold compression
•48 시간까지는수술직후가능하면지속(continuoscryo48-72중요)
•이후에는20분5회운동후, 잠들기전
•Elastic compression + elevation
•무릎주변elastic compression  + 다리심장보다높게
•누워서elevation
•Medication
•Acetaminophen + prn Opiod
•NSAIDs

In-hospital stage(pod#1~3) 
•Protection & Wound care
•Knee brace : 0 extension
•EOD dressing
•DVT prevention
•Ankle pump : 20회1시간1세트, 깨어있는동안반복
•Ankle/hipROM : pain-free 내, hip abduction, adduction 10회3세트/일
•조기보행POD1 부터

In-hospital stage(pod#1~3) 
•PWB with 2 crutch
•POD 0-1 짧은거리(병실내)
•POD1-3 복도보행10-20m하루5회
•Gait full extension
•목표는퇴원시2 crutch 안전한heel-toe gait
※ meniscusrepair
-WB: NWB or PWB 2–4주→ in-hospital에서는WB는toe-touch정도만.
-WB 상태에서**45–90°flexion**까지내려가는동작은금지.
※ meniscusrootrepair
-WB: in-hospital에서는WB gait연습이아니라, 목발사용법·비체중부하gaiteducation위주.
※ ACL + MLKI (PCL/MCL/LCL/PLC)
-TTWB with brace locked in extension가기본.
-보행시varus/valgus, hyperextension이나오지않게보조.

In-hospital stage(pod#1~3) 
•Early ROM
•Extension
•Heel prop : 10분3회/일
•Prone hang : 10분3회/일
•Flexion
•Heel slide : 10회하루5회통증3./10 이하면각도증가(70도시작)
•Sitting knee flexion : 반대다리보조10회3세트하루3회
※ Meniscusrepair
-Heelslide/ sittingflexion은0–90°
※ Meniscusrootrepair
-0–60°(UVA, McCarty, Chahla등)
※ PCL
-flexionROM 시tibiaposteriorshear(뒤꿈치로밀며굽히기) x.
※ PLC
-hyperextensionx 0°까지만extension

In-hospital stage(pod#1~3) 
•Quad activation
•Quad set : 10초유지10회3세트(깨어나있을때2-3시간마다)
•SLR : extensorlag없이quad 가능할때시작/ brace lock
•10회3세트하루1회45도까지들어올린후3-5초버티고천천히내리기(*MGH quadset5-10회시간당SLR 5회시간당매우자주시행
권장)
•NMES rehab

In-hospital stage(pod#1~3) 
•WB 
•Isolated ACL : WBAT with crutches, brace 0-90 
•Meniscus repair : toe touch brace 0-90
•Meniscus root repair : NWB with crutches brace –90
•Cartilage : NWB 
•MLKI : NWB 

Phase 1 check point –Pod 2wk Criteria 
based 
•Objective 를충족해야다음phase로
•Pain & Effusion
•Rest pain ≤ 3/10
•Knee effusion: none–trace
•Exercise/보행후다음날effusion 악화없음
•(Ref: Aspetar ACL CPG 2023)
•ROM
•Passive extension = contralateral (±2°, 
hyperextension 포함)
•Passive flexion ≥ 110°(Ref: van Grinsven 2010, 
MGH protocol) (meniscus repair 90, root repair 60 cartilage 90 이면pass)
•Quadriceps activation / strength
•SLR (supine) 10회이상, extensor lag 없음
•Chair sit-to-stand 10회,
•(Ref: Lepley 2015 review on AMI, Shaw et al., 
Aspetar & MGH ACL protocol)
•Gait 
•WBAT로full WB 가능
•Heel-toe gait, heel strike에서full extension
•보조도구: 최소1 crutch or no device로안전
보행가능
•(Ref:, Shelbourne accelerated protocol, Fan 
2022 review)
•Symptom
•No giving-way episodes
•IKDC/KOOS-ADL 항목에서걷기/계단/앉았다
일어나기severe limitation 없음
•(Ref: Aspetar CPG, Panther RTS consensus)
※Meniscus repair / root / MLKI
-“WBAT로full WB” 기준은충족하지못해도됨
–해당경우에는“허용된WB 범위내에서통증/부종없이gait 가능”.
iACL: Check point 달성시brace off 고려(quad control, SLR ok)
Meniscus repair : 0-90 허용WB시lock
Root repair : 0-90 허용WB시lock
Cartilage : 0-90 허용NWB 최소4주이후PWB
MLKI : 0-90 허용NWB ~ TTWB 최소4주이후PWB => FWB 

POST HAB stage –phase 2A : 
Intermediate (strength)
•Goal : Quad, hamstring strength / balance / 일상생활복귀
#1 Strengthrecovery 
•CKC 
•Chair squat –주3 2~3세트8-12회(10-12회힘든정도) flexion < 60
•Step up –15-20cm 주3 3세트8-12회
•Forward lunge –주3 2-3세트10회flexion 30-45도-> 3-4세트8-12회60-70도
※ Meniscusrepair
-Forwardlunge는3개월이전에는shallowrange(30–45°)까지만.
※ Meniscusrootrepair
-0–6주까지CKC squat/legpress/lunge금지, 이후에도0–60°까지만저중량.
※ PCL/MLKI
-WB 제한기간엔CKC 금지, 이후에도0–45°범위에서부터시작.
*Aspetar,vanMelick : squat, lunge, step up in 0-70

POST HAB stage –phase 2A : 
Intermediate (strength)
•Goal : Quad, hamstring strength / balance / 일상생활복귀
#1 Strengthrecovery 
•OKC
•Seated knee extension –주3 2~3세트10-15회/증상에따라조절90~45 -> 8
주이후90~10,15 / Theraband적용(low load, pain free 일때적용)
•SLR phase1 계속병행
Aspetar,vanMelick : OKC 90-40 / Wilk 등biomechanical review OKC ext40-0 high load -> 
graft strain 상승하므로low load (테라밴드) 적용

POST HAB stage –phase 2A : 
Intermediate (strength)
•Goal : Quad, hamstring strength / balance / 일상생활복귀
#1 Strengthrecovery 
•Hamstring & Hip 
•Bridge주3 3세트10–15회
•side lying hip abduction phase 1 계속병행
※ Meniscusrepair/root/cartilage/ PCL
-bridgedeep flexion, 고중량위험및posterior shear 위험있어제외

POST HAB stage –phase 2A : 
Intermediate (strength)
•Goal : Quad, hamstring strength / balance / 일상생활복귀
#1 Strengthrecovery 
•Core stability
•Dead bug 주32-3세트8-12회
•Front plank, sideplank 주3 3세트20초-> 45~60초까지증가

POST HAB stage –phase 2B : 
Intermediate (neuromascular)
#1 strength part 함께진행
#2 Balance
•Single leg + Mini squat 주3 3세트20-30초15-30도유지
•Y-balanceanteriorposteromedialposterolateral주3 각방향3세트5
회
•Perturbationdrill –wobble board
•Singlelegstance 30초이상+minisquat 가안정적, NRS 3점이하, noeffusion 
인시기(대략8주후)
•책상앞double leg mini squat 자세주3 3세트30초
※ Meniscusrepair
-ybalance10주이후
※ Meniscusrootrepair/ cartilage
-Y balance 12주이후
※ PCL/MLKI
-항상valgus varus 주의perturbation sagittal 위주, frontal 은12주이후

POST HAB stage –phase 2 : 
Intermediate (neuromascular)
#3 preparingRTS
•평지빠르게걷기–통증3/10 effusion 악화없이20-30분주3-5회
RPE3,4(말은되나숨찬정도)
•Jogging은phase 3 부터brisk walking 만시행
※Meniscus repair/root
-Brisk walking, bike는허용가능하나, running/jogging은최소4개월이후
※ MLKI
-경사/불규칙지면에서brisk walking은늦게시작, 평지treadmill부터.

Phase 2 check point –Pod 10wk Criteria 
based 
•Objective 를충족해야다음phase로
•Movement Quality (single leg squat / step down)
•수술측single-leg squat 0–60°, step-down 15–20cm에서
•눈에띄는dynamic valgus, hip drop, trunk side bend, 
giving way 없음
•(Ref: Aspetar ACL CPG 2023)
•Balance / Hop 
•Hop test, Y-Balance : LSI ≥ 80% (hop test 의경우ACL specific 한
인자로보고되어중요지표)
•(Ref : Rambaud AJM et al. Br J Sports Med )
•Subjective 
•계단, 빠른걷기, 가벼운방향전환에서giving way 느낌
없음
•무릎불안감때문에일상/가벼운운동을피하는정도가
아님
•IKDC / KOOS-ADL / ACL-RSI fail 수준이상
(Ref : Logerstedt D et al. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther.
2014, Aspetar CPG). ACL-RSI specific 한지표로중요
•Pain (통증)
•일상+ Phase 2 운동시NRS/VAS <2/10
•(Ref : Rambaud AJM et al. Br J Sports Med )
•Effusion (부종)
•운동후에도다음날둘레증가/열감거의없음
(Ref: Aspetar ACL CPG 2023)
•ROM
•extension: 0°
•flexion≥ 95%
•(Ref: Aspetar ACL CPG 2023)
•Strength (Quad / Hamstring)
•Quad & HS LSI ≥ 80%
•(Ref: Aspetar ACL CPG 2023)
Meniscus repair : braceoff
Cartilage : 8-10주이후brace off 
MLKI : 최소3개월까지brace 유지

Phase 2 check point –Pod 10wk Criteria 
based 
•Objective 를충족해야다음phase로
•Meniscus repair
•Strength:quad/HS70%
•Hop test 실시하지않고Y-balance실시
•Phase 3 로진행하되multi directional hop 나
cutting 이포함된방향전환은16주이후실시
•Root repair / cartilage (MicroFx)
•16주에check point를실시
•Strength LSI70%
•MLKI
•StengthLSI 70%
•Hoptest 실시하지않고Y-balance 실시
•Phase 3로진행하되plyo, agility 에서double leg 
위주로실시한다
•RTS 는12개월이후진행

POST HAB stage –phase 3 : Late
(pre RTS)
•Goal : Running, strength , Return to activity -> RTS
#1 Running
•Phase 3 1-2 week:1분조깅2분걷기10세트주3회(8~10km/h, 대화
가능한페이스)
•3-4 week: 5분조깅1분걷기4세트주3회증상없을때속도,시간증가
•5 week later : 조깅+ 러닝
•매세션다음날effusion, pain 체크하여조절
※ Meniscusrepair/root
-Running시작시점은최소4개월이후, rootrepair는더지연될수있음.
-Downhillrunning, cutting포함된러닝프로그램은6개월이후.
※ MLKI
-고속intervalrunning, 불규칙지면달리기는더늦게9 개월이후

POST HAB stage –phase 3 : Late
(pre RTS)
#2 Strength
•Single leg squat : 3-4 세트8-12회주3회70-80도
•Single leg bridge : 3세트10-15회상부에서1-2초유지주3회
•OKC knee extension : 탄력밴드사용점진적부하90-> 10 천천히펴
고1초정지3세트10-15회주3회(pain ,crepitus -> step down)
.※ Meniscusrootrepair
-Single-leg, 0–45°범위에서시작.
※ MLKI
-Single-legsquat중varus/valgus크게발생하면이전phase의double-legtask로회귀.

POST HAB stage –phase 3 : Late
(pr

Rehabilitation protocol 
after ACL recon at
postop 
노원을지대병원류마티스내과신정민교수

Stages
Preoperative Rehabilitation stage
진단후~ 수술(4-6주)  *(Shaarani et al., Am J 
Sports Med 2013 6주, Eitzen et al. 프로그램(Br J 
Sports Med 2010) 5주로시행했고다만spetar 
ACL CPG, van Melick CPG 등에서consensus 가
없다고명시)
Prehab 4-6주는이상적이나acute case (2주안
수술의경우) 대부분논문에서제시하는effusion 
최대한감소, full passive extension, quad 
activation (slr w/o lag) 를목표로한다
Chronic case의경우바로check point 검사후
짧게term을가져간다
In-hospital stage
POD #1 ~ #3 
Post-hab stage
After discharge ~ Return to sports
이프로토콜은isolated ACL reconstruction을기본으로하며, meniscus/다른ligament/cartilage 수술이동반된경우각슬라이드하단의‘동반손상주의사항’을따라야함.

POST HAB stage
•3 Phases –f/u 날짜에objective 달성확인후다음phase (*van Melic, Aspetar) 
•Phase 1 : Acute phase (pain control , swelling , full extension, functional 
ADLs) 
•Check point #1 –POD 2wk
•Phase 2 : Intermediate phase (full ROM , good strength & movement)
•2A (strength part) POD6wk
•2B (neuromascularpart) POD 10wk
•Check point #2 –POD 10wk
•Phase 3 : Late phase (Running -> sport participation -> full RTS with 
low risk injury) 
•Check point #3 –POD 10mo

POST HAB stage (WB time based)
Injury pattern0–2주2–4주4–6주6–8주8–10주

WBAT with crut
ches
FWB, gait norm
alizing
FWBFWBFWB
+ Meniscus repairTTWB 10–20%PWB 25–50%FWB, CKC 0–45°FWB, CKC 0–60°FWB
+ Root repairNWBNWBPWB 25–50%FWB, CKC 0–45°FWB, CKC 0–60°
+ CartilageNWB or TTWBNWB or TTWBPWB (시작)PWB→FWBFWB, CKC 0–45°
+ MLKI

h only)

10)

POST HAB stage –phase 1 : Acute
•Goal : Quiet knee / full extension / functional ADLs (Aspetar, MGH)
#1 Control pain & effusion 
•Cryotherapy + compression + elevation
•얼음팩수술부위주변20분하루3회
•Elastic compression
•Elevation

POST HAB stage –phase 1 : Acute
#2 Early ROM (통증3/10 이상이면조절)
extension
•Heel prop/ prone hang 3분유지5세트5회
•무릎뒤배개는금지
flexion 
•Heel slide 10-15회2-3 세트하루3-5회
※ Meniscusrepair
-Heelslide, sittingflexion은90°이내
※ Meniscusrootrepair
-초기3주: **0–60°**
※ PCL
-발뒤꿈치로바닥/침대를강하게밀며tibia를뒤로미는패턴피하기.
※ PLC
-Pronehang/heelprop에서hyperextensionx

POST HAB stage –phase 1 : Acute
#3 Quadriceps 활성화
•Quad set 5초수축10회3세트하루5회
•SLR 10회3세트하루2회
•extensor lag 없을때시행
•Hip flexion prone extension
#4 Hip/CoreStrengthening
•Silde-lying hip abduction 10회3세트
•Clamshall10회3세트

POST HAB stage –phase 1 : Acute
#5 Gait Training & Weight bearing
•Carefully progressed early WB
•Isolated ACLR 기준grafttype,동반손상에따라조절
•Symptom based
•보조기+ 목발
•ADL범위내보행
•목표는조기정상heel-toe gait
•Full knee extension at heel strike
※ meniscusrepair
-TTWB/PWB로보행(발끝터치수준).
-보행중45–90°까지굽혀지는deepstance, 계단반복은피함.
※ meniscusrootrepair
-NWB 기간(보통4–6주)에는보행패턴교육중심, 실제WB gait연습은하지않음.
※ ACL + MLKI
-brace locked ext상태에서straight line gait만, 방향전환·side step은금지.

POST HAB stage –phase 1 : Acute
#6 Patellar Mobilization
•Patellofemoral stiffness 예방, pain 감소, ROM 회복촉진
•봉합부위압박은x
※Meniscus root / cartilage tibial plateau 수술
-Tibial plateau 바로위를강하게누르거나, 절개부위에직접압박하지않도록주
의.

POST HAB stage –phase 1 : Acute
•WB 
•Isolated ACL : WBAT with crutches, brace 0-90 
•Meniscus repair : PWB 25-50% brace 0-90
•Meniscus root repair : NWB with crutches brace 0–90
•Cartilage : NWB or TTWB

Phase 1 check point –Pod 2wk Criteria 
based

•Exercise/보행후다음날effusion 악화없음
•(Ref: Aspetar ACL CPG 2023)

•Passive extension = contralateral (±2°, 
hyperextension 포함)
•Passive flexion ≥ 110°(Ref: van Grinsven 2010, 
MGH protocol) (meniscus repair 90, root repair 60 cartilage 90 이면pass)
•Quadriceps activation / strength

•Chair sit-to-stand 10회,
•(Ref: Lepley 2015 review on AMI, Shaw et al., 
Aspetar & MGH ACL protocol)
•Gait

•Heel-toe gait, heel strike에서full extension
•보조도구: 최소1 crutch or no device로안전
보행가능

2022 review)
•Symptom
•No giving-way episodes
•IKDC/KOOS-ADL 항목에서걷기/계단/앉았다
일어나기severe limitation 없음
•(Ref: Aspetar CPG, Panther RTS consensus)
※Meniscus repair / root / MLKI
-“WBAT로full WB” 기준은충족하지못해도됨
–해당경우에는“허용된WB 범위내에서통증/부종없이gait 가능”.
iACL: Check point 달성시brace off 고려(quad control, SLR ok)
Meniscus repair : 0-90 허용WB시lock
Root repair : 0-90 허용WB시lock
Cartilage : 0-90 허용NWB 최소4주이후PWB
MLKI : 0-90 허용NWB ~ TTWB 최소4주이후PWB => FWB 

POST HAB stage –phase 2A : 
Intermediate (strength)
•Goal : Quad, hamstring strength / balance / 일상생활복귀
#1 Strengthrecovery 
•CKC 
•Chair squat –주3 2~3세트8-12회(10-12회힘든정도) flexion < 60
•Step up –15-20cm 주3 3세트8-12회
•Forward lunge –주3 2-3세트10회flexion 30-45도-> 3-4세트8-12회60-70도

※ Meniscusrootrepair
-0–6주까지CKC squat/legpress/lunge금지, 이후에도0–60°까지만저중량.
※ PCL/MLKI
-WB 제한기간엔CKC 금지, 이후에도0–45°범위에서부터시작.
*Aspetar,vanMelick : squat, lunge, step up in 0-70

#1 Strengthrecovery 
•OKC
•Seated knee extension –주3 2~3세트10-15회/증상에따라조절90~45 -> 8
주이후90~10,15 / Theraband적용(low load, pain free 일때적용)

graft strain 상승하므로low load (테라밴드) 적용

#1 Strengthrecovery 
•Hamstring & Hip 
•Bridge주3 3세트10–15회

POST HAB stage –phase 2A : 
Intermediate (strength)

#1 Strengthrecovery

•Dead bug 주32-3세트8-12회

Intermediate (neuromascular)
#1 strength part 함께진행
#2 Balance
•Single leg + Mini squat 주3 3세트20-30초15-30도유지

회
•Perturbationdrill –wobble board

인시기(대략8주후)
•책상앞double leg mini squat 자세주3 3세트30초
※ Meniscusrepair
-ybalance10주이후
※ Meniscusrootrepair/ cartilage
-Y balance 12주이후
※ PCL/MLKI
-항상valgus varus 주의perturbation sagittal 위주, frontal 은12주이후

POST HAB stage –phase 2 : 
Intermediate (neuromascular)
#3 preparingRTS

files.name: 전문가활용보고서_강여경251201...pdf
files.text:
Cover Letter 
Cover Letter (Resubmission) 
Journal of Clinical Medicine (MDPI) 
 
Dear Editors of the Journal of Clinical Medicine, 
We are pleased to resubmit our revised manuscript entitled: 
“Effects of Closed and Open Kinetic Chain Exercises on Functional Recovery After Total Knee 
Arthroplasty in Elderly Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial” 
for your consideration for publication in the Journal of Clinical Medicine. 
 
Summary of the Study 
This prospective, single-blind randomized controlled trial directly compared the effects of a 12-week closed 
kinetic chain exercise (CKCE) program and an open kinetic chain exercise (OKCE) program with a real-world 
standard-care group (non-protocolized activity) in elderly women following total knee arthroplasty (TKA). 
We assessed multiple clinically relevant outcomes, including muscle strength, static and dynamic balance, range 
of motion (ROM), gait performance, knee function (WOMAC), and pain (VAS). 
Our findings indicate that both CKCE and OKCE led to significant improvements in lower limb strength, ROM, 
and balance over time, compared with standard care. Notably, the CKCE group achieved greater gains in knee 
extension strength and gait performance, suggesting potential modality-specific benefits for this patient 
demographic. 
 
Major Revisions in Response to Reviewer Comments 
In response to the reviewers’ constructive feedback, we have undertaken substantial revisions to improve clarity, 
methodological transparency, and clinical relevance: 
1. Clarified Study Rationale – Expanded explanation for including only elderly women and for selecting 
a real-world standard-care comparator, highlighting the clinical relevance in settings with limited 
rehabilitation resources. 
2. Enhanced Novelty Statement – Clearly distinguished our design from the 2023 IJERP study, 
emphasizing strict CKC/OKC separation, consistent two-week post-TKA initiation, RPE-based 
progressive loading, and focus on an under-represented population. 
3. Improved Statistical Reporting – Added effect sizes and 95% confidence intervals for all primary and 
secondary outcomes, and aligned statistical results consistently between text, tables, and figures. 
4. Addressed Sample Size Concerns – Detailed the a priori power calculation for the primary outcome, 
acknowledged the potential for Type II error in secondary outcomes, and discussed this explicitly in 
the manuscript. 
5. Expanded Literature Context – Incorporated deeper comparisons with studies on proprioception, gait 
kinematics, and neuromuscular control following TKA; discussed clinically meaningful trends in 
WOMAC and VAS despite non-significance. 

All changes are marked in the tracked-changes version with below “[Reviewer Comments and Author 
Reponses]”, with a clean copy provided for publication consideration. 
 
Statement of Originality and Ethical Compliance 
The manuscript has not been published previously and is not under consideration elsewhere. All authors have 
read and approved the final version, and there are no conflicts of interest to declare. 
The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of G University Hospital (IRB No: GDIRB2022-
095) and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Written informed consent was obtained 
from all participants prior to enrollment. 
 
We believe that the extensive revisions made in direct response to reviewer comments have strengthened the 
manuscript and its contribution to the field. We thank the editors and reviewers for their valuable insights, which 
have enhanced the rigor and clarity of our work. We respectfully submit this revised version for your 
consideration and look forward to the opportunity to contribute to the Journal of Clinical Medicine. 
Dear Journal of Clinical Medicine Editorial Office 
 
We are pleased to submit our manuscript entitled “Effects of Closed and Open Kinetic Chain Exercises on 
Functional Recovery After Total Knee Arthroplasty in Elderly Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial” for 
your consideration for publication in Journal of Clinical Medicine. 
 
In this randomized controlled trial, we investigated and compared the effects of 12-week closed kinetic chain 
exercises (CKCE) and open kinetic chain exercises (OKCE) on functional recovery parameters in elderly 
women who had undergone total knee arthroplasty (TKA). Our study assessed multiple clinically relevant 
outcomes, including muscle strength, balance, range of motion (ROM), gait performance, knee function, and 
pain. 
 
The findings demonstrated that both CKCE and OKCE significantly improved lower limb strength, ROM, and 
balance. Notably, the closed kinetic chain group showed superior improvements in knee extension strength and 
gait performance, suggesting that CKCE may offer specific advantages during postoperative rehabilitation in 
this population. 
 
To our knowledge, this study is among the few to directly compare these two rehabilitation strategies using 
comprehensive functional assessments in elderly female TKA patients. We believe that our findings contribute 
meaningful insights to the optimization of postoperative rehabilitation protocols for improving long-term 
functional outcomes in this growing patient population. 
 
In this paper, we investigated the indicators for checking the degree of improvement through rehabilitation after 
TKA in detail and tried to systematically describe the results and interpretation of each research item. Therefore, 
the number of words in this paper exceeded the word limit of 3,000.  

 
The manuscript has not been published and is not under consideration elsewhere. All authors have approved the 
submission and there are no conflicts of interest to declare. 
 
We appreciate your consideration of our work and look forward to the opportunity to contribute to your journal. 
 
Sincerely, 
Jae Ang Sim, MD., PhD. 
 
Department of Orthopaedics Surgery, Gil Medical Center, Gachon University College of Medicine, Incheon, 
Republic of Korea, 21565 
Fax: +82-32-423-3384 
Tel: +82-32-460-3384 
Cell: +82-10-6221-6957 
Email: sim_ja@gilhospital.com 
 
Byung Hoon Lee, MD., PhD. 
Department of Orthopaedics Surgery, Gil Medical Center, Gachon University College of Medicine, Incheon, 
Republic of Korea 
Fax: +82-32-423-3384 
Tel: +82-32-460-3384 
Cell: +82-10-8680-8014 
Email: oselite@naver.com 
  

[Reviewer Comments and Author Reponses] 
 
Reviewer#1 
[Comments and Suggestions for Authors] 
Thank you for the opportunity to review the paper titled: "Comparison of Closed and Open Kinetic Chain 
Exercises Following Total Knee Arthroplasty in Elderly Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial." The need to 
improve rehabilitation outcomes after total knee replacement (TKR) is a critical issue in geriatric and orthopedic 
care, especially given the increasing number of patients undergoing this procedure each year. However, 
comparing rehabilitation protocols to a no-intervention group lacks relevance, as the effectiveness of 
rehabilitation following TKR is already well established and ethically necessary. Furthermore, this study 
presents a very similar design to another paper published in 2023 in the IJERP journal (Effect of Combined 
Kinematic Chain Exercise on Physical Function, Balance Ability, and Gait in Patients with Total Knee 
Arthroplasty: A Single-Blind Randomized Controlled Trial). The interventions used in both the experimental 
and control groups are extremely similar, and the outcomes evaluated are essentially the same. From my point of 
view, this study does not contribute any novel knowledge, and its findings do not advance the current 
understanding in the field. Additionally, the small sample size—only 12 participants per group—further limits 
the potential impact of the study. Evaluating similar rehabilitation approaches with such limited data does not 
provide meaningful insights or strengthen the existing literature. 
 
 
[Author responses] 
*Reviewer Comment 1: 
Comparing rehabilitation protocols to a no-intervention group lacks relevance, as the effectiveness of 
rehabilitation following TKA is already well established and ethically necessary. 
*Author response: 
We understand the concern. In this study, the “standard care” (control) group was not truly ‘no rehabilitation’, 
but rather represents the common real-world scenario in certain outpatient or resource-limited settings where 
patients receive post-discharge advice but not structured, protocolized exercise. This inclusion allows us to 
quantify the incremental benefit of CKC and OKC interventions relative to the actual care many patients 
receive, thereby addressing an implementation gap in the literature. 
 
*Modification to Materials and Methods  
“Study Design and Rationale[YK1] 
This study was a prospective, single-blind, randomized controlled trial designed to specifically compare the 
efficacy of distinct closed kinetic chain (CKCG) and open kinetic chain (OKCG) exercise programs against a 
standard-care (unregulated physical activity) group following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) in older women. 
The inclusion of a control group receiving standard post-discharge care (no protocolized exercise intervention) 
reflects real-world clinical practices in certain settings, particularly in resource-limited or outpatient 
community contexts where not all patients consistently access structured rehabilitation. This approach enables 
assessment of the incremental gains achievable with well-defined kinetic chain interventions, which remains a 
clinically relevant and under-investigated question. 
Unlike previous studies, which often compare types of exercises but do not benchmark them to non-protocolized 
usual care, this trial was specifically powered and designed to quantify real-world gains for each exercise 
modality relative to standard post-discharge experience.” 

 
*Reviewer Comment 2: 
The study design is very similar to a 2023 IJERP paper... The interventions and outcomes are essentially the 
same. 
*Author response: 
Our study differs from the 2023 IJERP paper in several important ways: 
1. The IJERP study investigated a combined kinetic chain protocol vs guideline-based rehab, whereas our trial 
strictly separated CKC and OKC protocols and also included a real-world usual care group for comparison. 
2. Interventions were initiated consistently two weeks post-TKA, with progressive loading using RPE-based 
adaptation. 
3. The program was developed specifically for elderly East Asian women, an underrepresented group in 
Western-centric literature. 
4. The aim was to evaluate both modalities independently against standard care to provide direct, actionable data 
for clinical decision-making. 
*Modification to Materials and Methods 
“Interventions[YK2] 
Importantly, while elements of the CKC and OKC exercise protocols are based on established rehabilitative 
approaches, the sequencing, timing (initiation at 2 weeks post-op), exercise progression patterns, and specific 
adaptation to the local patient population constitute novel aspects not directly replicated in previous literature. 
The control group’s unregulated activity reflects the actual post-TKA experience for a non-select group of 
patients.” 
*Modification to Discussion 
“1. Comparison to Previous Literature and Novelty[YK3] 
While the efficacy of rehabilitation after TKA is well documented, the specific comparative value of rigorously 
structured CKC and OKC programs relative to standard outpatient care (where supervision may be limited) has 
remained insufficiently quantified. Notably, the commonly referenced 2023 study in IJERP (see reviewer’s 
comment) tested different parameters: it investigated a combined kinematic chain protocol versus standard 
guideline-based rehabilitation, whereas our trial implemented a strict separation of CKC and OKC 
interventions, with all protocols initiated two weeks post-TKA, and directly compared both to an unregulated 
standard-care c

files.text:
[October 1
st
, 2025] 
Prof. Dr. Rafal Marszalek 
Chief Editor 
Scientific Reports 
 
Dear Dr. Marszalek: 
 
I wish to resubmit the manuscript titled “Comparison of Closed and Open Kinetic Chain Exercises 
Following Total Knee Arthroplasty in Elderly Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial” The 
manuscript Submission ID is 470c15a6-3e01-4bcc-8e2b-69ea217a7314. 
 
We thank you and the reviewers for your thoughtful suggestions and insights. The manuscript has 
benefited from these insightful suggestions. I look forward to working with you and the reviewers to 
move this manuscript closer to publication in Scientific Reports. 
 
The manuscript has been re-evaluated and the necessary changes have been made in accordance with 
the reviewers’ suggestions. This paper has gone through two rounds of review, and the details for 
each revision are added on the “Point-by point response to reviewers” file with colored text (blue 
text). 
 
Thank you for your consideration. I look forward to hearing from you.

1 
 
Comparison of Closed and Open Kinetic Chain Exercises Following Total Knee 1 
Arthroplasty in Elderly Women: A Randomized Controlled Trial 2 
 3 
Jae Ang Sim
1
, Kyeong-Sik Kong
1
, Moon Hee Kim
1
, Kyung Jin Kim
1
, Byung Hoon Lee
1

 4 
1 
Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Gachon University College of Medicine, Incheon, 5 
Republic of Korea 6 
 7 

email: oselite@naver.com  8 

2 
 
Abstract 9 
This study investigated the comparative effects of a 12-week closed kinetic chain (CKC) 10 
versus open kinetic chain (OKC) exercise program on muscle strength, balance, range of 11 
motion (ROM), gait, knee function, and pain in elderly women after total knee arthroplasty 12 
(TKA). 13 
Thirty-six elderly women post-TKA were randomized into three groups: CKC exercise 14 
(n=12), OKC exercise (n=12), and control (n=12). The intervention groups followed a 15 
structured 12-week exercise regimen. Outcome measures included knee flexion and extension 16 
strength, single-leg standing time, Timed Up and Go (TUG) test, six-minute walk test 17 
(6MWT), ROM, Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index 18 
(WOMAC), and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores for pain. 19 
All groups exhibited significant improvements in knee extension strength over time. The 20 
CKC group showed a 122% increase (mean difference = 10.8 Nm; 95% CI, 7.8 to 13.8; p < 21 
0.001), outperforming the OKC (102%) and control groups (97%). ROM and static and 22 
dynamic balance improved similarly across groups without significant differences. Gait 23 
function, measured by the 6MWT, increased significantly in all groups, with the CKC group 24 
demonstrating the greatest gains. No significant differences were observed among groups for 25 
pain (VAS) and function (WOMAC) scores. 26 
Both CKC and OKC exercise programs effectively enhance lower limb strength, ROM, and 27 
balance following TKA in elderly women. However, CKC exercises confer superior benefits 28 
in knee extension strength and gait performance, suggesting their preferential use in post-29 
TKA rehabilitation protocols. 30 
 31 

3 
 
Keywords: Total Knee Arthroplasty, Closed Kinetic Chain Exercise, Osteoarthritis, Open 32 
Kinetic Chain Exercise, Rehabilitation, Randomized controlled trial 33 
  34 

4 
 
Introduction 35 
Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) has been proven to be highly effective in providing pain relief 36 
and improving physical function, thereby enhancing the quality of life of patients 
[1,2]
. This 37 
surgical procedure can effectively alleviate pain, bone deformity, and joint contracture due to 38 
severe arthritis, and can also lead to an improvement in lower limb function. However, 39 
postoperative muscle atrophy and neuromuscular deficits may occur 
[3]
, which can negatively 40 
impact the ability to perform functional movements, such as climbing stairs and walking. 41 
Bade et al. reported that older adults who underwent TKA experienced an 18% decrease in 42 
walking speed, a 51% decrease in stair climbing speed, and a 40% reduction in quadriceps 43 
femoris strength within a year after surgery compared to older adults who did not undergo the 44 
procedure 
[4]
. Therefore, it is recommended that patients begin exercising as early as possible 45 
after TKA to achieve optimal surgical outcomes, including pain relief and improved lower 46 
limb function 
[5]
. 47 
The post-surgical rehabilitation program for TKA can vary based on the environment, but it 48 
typically focuses on pain and edema management, restoring the range of motion (ROM) of 49 
the knee joint, strengthening the lower limbs, achieving a normal walking pattern, and 50 
training for functional activities
[6,7]
. 51 
Rehabilitation exercises following joint replacement surgery are currently primarily based on 52 
open kinetic chain exercises 
[4]
. However, these exercises are not considered functional-based 53 
due to their lack of similarity to everyday movements, and they may increase shear forces on 54 
the artificial joint 
[8,9]
. To improve clinically practical function in patients undergoing TKA, it 55 
is necessary to consider closed kinetic chain exercises, which can enhance balance, increase 56 
joint pressure to effectively stimulate proprioceptive systems 
[7]
, and improve muscle 57 

5 
 
strength, power, stability, and balance, thus qualifying as functional exercises. However, there 58 
is a lack of research on early closed kinetic chain exercises and functional exercises following 59 
TKA. Therefore, there is a need for studies to validate these approaches, as changes in 60 
rehabilitation methods are not currently supported by sufficient evidence 
[8]
. This study aimed 61 
to examine the effects of 12-week closed kinetic chain exercise programs and open kinetic 62 
chain exercise programs on muscle strength, balance, ROM, and functional gait ability in 63 
patients who underwent TKA. In particular, few studies have directly compared the 64 
effectiveness of early CKC and OKC interventions against real-world usual care in elderly 65 
East Asian women, despite their high prevalence of TKA and unique rehabilitation 66 
needs. This study was designed to address this gap by directly comparing strictly separated 67 
CKC and OKC exercise programs, initiated 2 weeks post-TKA, with a standard-care group, 68 
and by evaluating multiple functional outcomes including muscle strength, balance, range of 69 
motion, gait performance, and patient-reported function. 70 
 71 
Results 72 
Comparison of Measurement Items Among Groups 73 
Knee Extension Strength 74 
Rehabilitation exercise following TKA resulted in significant changes in knee extension 75 
strength over time (p < 0.001) and between groups (p = 0.013). Post-hoc comparisons showed 76 
that CKCG demonstrated a 122% increase from baseline (8.80 ± 3.94 Nm) to 3 months 77 
(19.60 ± 4.50 Nm, p < 0.001), OKCG a 102% increase (7.87 ± 3.62 to 15.90 ± 3.10 Nm, p < 78 
0.001), and CG a 97% increase (6.75 ± 2.30 to 13.13 ± 3.55 Nm, p < 0.001) (Fig. 2A). At 3 79 

6 
 
months, mean differences from baseline were: CKCG +10.8 Nm (95% CI: 7.8–13.8, Cohen’s 80 
d = 2.48), OKCG +8.03 Nm (95% CI: 5.2–10.7, d = 2.16), and CG +6.38 Nm (95% CI: 3.6–81 
9.1, d = 1.92). Between-group analysis revealed significantly greater gains in CKCG than 82 
OKCG at 2 months (17.1 ± 4.4 vs. 14.3 ± 3.4 Nm, p = 0.029) and 3 months (19.5 ± 4.5 vs. 83 
16.5 ± 3.8 Nm, p = 0.032). Both CKCG and OKCG outperformed CG at 3 months (p = 0.018 84 
and p = 0.021, respectively). 85 
Knee Flexion Strength 86 
Pre-test values significantly influenced knee flexion strength as a covariate (F = 25.196, p < 87 
0.001), but no group-by-time interaction was observed (p = 0.463). Main effect analysis 88 
showed no significant differences between groups (p = 0.213) or over time (p = 0.096). At 3 89 
months, flexion strength increased by 36% in CKCG (11.6 ± 3.9 to 15.8 ± 3.8 Nm), 46% in 90 
OKCG (7.4 ± 2.1 to 10.9 ± 1.5 Nm), and 57% in CG (6.5 ± 2.3 to 10.2 ± 3.7 Nm) (Fig. 2B). 91 
Balance Performance 92 
No significant group-by-time interaction was detected for static or dynamic balance (p = 93 
0.348). Static balance (single-leg stance) improved by 202% in CKCG (17.67 ± 17.07 to 94 
53.45 ± 44.33 s), 215% in OKCG (8.44 ± 7.66 to 26.61 ± 28.59 s), and 458% in CG (4.51 ± 95 
4.33 to 25.21 ± 35.97 s). TUG performance showed no significant group (p = 0.427) or time 96 
(p = 0.185) effects, yet all groups improved over time: CKCG decreased by 35% (10.47 ± 97 
1.61 to 6.84 ± 0.44 s), OKCG by 52% (16.74 ± 9.49 to 8.16 ± 2.76 s), and CG by 40% (13.26 98 
± 4.60 to 8.05 ± 1.17 s). 99 
Knee Flexion Range of Motion (ROM) 100 
Flexion ROM significantly increased over time in all groups (p < 0.001), with no significant 101 

7 
 
group-by-time interaction (p = 0.299). At 3 months, ROM improved by 31% in CKCG (79.1 102 
± 12.3 to 103.7 ± 17.7°), 56% in OKCG (58.1 ± 16.6 to 90.7 ± 14.2°), and 45% in CG (65.3 ± 103 
30.7 to 95.2 ± 20.5°). 104 
Six-Minute Walk Test (6MWT) 105 
Walking capacity significantly increased over time for all groups. CKCG achieved greater 106 
improvements at each time point compared with OKCG and CG. At 3 months, CKCG 107 
increased by 91% from baseline (265.1 ± 121.3 to 508.0 ± 67.6 m, p < 0.001), OKCG by 108 
99% (213.1 ± 119.2 to 425.3 ± 91.7 m, p < 0.001), and CG by 61% (242.2 ± 127.6 to 391.1 ± 109 
107.0 m, p < 0.001) (Table 5). Between-group comparisons indicated that CKCG 110 
outperformed OKCG at both 2 months (p = 0.028) and 3 months (p = 0.024), and CG at 2 111 
months (p = 0.006) and 3 months (p = 0.007). No difference was observed between OKCG 112 
and CG. (Fig. 3). 113 
Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) 114 
WOMAC scores showed no significant group-by-time interaction (p = 0.092), group 115 
differences (p = 0.876), or time effects (p = 0.140). At 3 months, CKCG decreased by 53% 116 
(35.2 ± 15.1 to 16.5 ± 10.0), OKCG by 45% (48.5 ± 18.8 to 26.3 ± 17.3), and CG by 48% 117 
(53.8 ± 17.7 to 28.0 ± 18.9). 118 
Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for Pain 119 
VAS scores showed no significant group-by-time interaction (p = 0.370) or main effects of 120 
group (p = 0.268) and time (p = 0.839). At 3 months, CKCG decreased by 64% (5.18 ± 1.72 121 
to 1.97 ± 1.76), and OKCG decreased by 67% from baseline (6.17 ± 2.21) at 3 months post-122 
intervention. 123 

8 
 
 124 
Discussion 125 
The primary objective of this study was to investigate the effects of a 12-week closed kinetic 126 
chain exercise program compared with an open chain program on muscle strength, balance, 127 
range of motion, gait, activities of daily living, and pain in older patients after knee 128 
arthroplasty. In the following discussion, these findings are interpreted in the context of their 129 
clinical significance and implications. 130 
Comparison to Previous Literature and Novelty 131 
While the benefits of rehabilitation following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) are well 132 
established, the comparative value of closed kinetic chain (CKC) and open kinetic chain 133 
(OKC) exercise programs relative to standard outpatient care remains insufficiently 134 
characterized. Most prior investigations have assessed combined chain protocols or 135 
guideline-based rehabilitation, rather than isolating CKC and OKC as distinct interventions. 136 
In contrast, the present trial implemented a strict separation of CKC and OKC programs, 137 
initiated uniformly at two weeks post-TKA, and compared them directly with an unstructured 138 
standard-care group. Furthermore, the program design—including session frequency, 139 
progression criteria based on ratings of perceived exertion, and individualized ad

files.name: 연구출장보고서_이병훈_251020~251025.pdf
files.text:
연구출장보고서
위와 같이 출장보고서를 제출합니다.
2025년 11월 7일
연구책임자   소속정형외과직급교수성명이병훈  이병훈
연구과제명디지털헬스케어 의료기기 실증지원 사업(임살실증분야) / 1연차
과제번호B0080826000920지원기관대구경북첨단의료산업진흥재단
총 연구기간2024.08.14 ~ 2025.12.311 차 연구기간2025.01.01 ~ 2025.12.31
지원사업명디지털헬스케어 의료기기 실증지원 사업 2차년도
소속직급 및 성명출장목적출장기간출장지
정형외과   교수 이병훈
-스포츠 의학 관련 병원, 연구기관 및
스포츠 훈련센터 방문
-학회(TOA Congress)참석 및 발표
2025.10.19~2025.10.26
(8일간)
대만
출장결과
이번 TOA Congress 참석을 통해 정형외과 및 스포츠 의학 분야의 최신 연구 동향과 기술 발전
방향을 확인할 수 있었으며, 특히 디지털 헬스케어 및 의료기기 실증지원 사업과 연계 가능한
임상 적용 사례들을 공유받는 유익한 기회가 되었다. 학회 발표를 통해 연구성과를 국제적으로
소개하고 관련 연구자들과의 학술적 교류를 확대할 수 있었다. 또한 대만의 스포츠 의학 관련
병원, 연구기관 및 훈련센터를 방문하여 현지의 스포츠 재활 프로그램과 임상 운영체계를 직접
확인하고 비교함으로써 향후 연구개발 및 임상실증에 참고할 수 있는 실질적인 인사이트를 얻
었다. 이번 출장을 통해 확보한 정보와 교류를 바탕으로 향후 디지털 헬스케어 기반 스포츠 의
학 연구 및 실증 사업의 고도화를 지속적으로 추진할 예정이다.
내역
출장기간:2025.10.19~2025.10.26/출장지:대만/목적:스포츠 의학 관련 병원, 연구기관 및 스포츠
훈련센터 방문&학회(TOA Congress)참석 및 발표
